vulnerability.title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
vulnerability.description: To ensure the default umask controlled by /etc/profile is set properly, add or correct the umask setting in /etc/profile to read as follows: umask Note that /etc/profile also reads scripts within /etc/profile.d directory. These scripts are also valid files to set umask value. Therefore, they should also be considered during the check and properly remediated, if necessary.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_accounts_user_umask='' readarray -t profile_files < <(find /etc/profile.d/ -type f -name '*.sh' -or -name 'sh.local') for file in "${profile_files[@]}" /etc/profile; do grep -qE '^[^#]*umask' "$file" && sed -i -E "s/^(\s*umask\s*)[0-7]+/\1$var_accounts_user_umask/g" "$file" done if ! grep -qrE '^[^#]*umask' /etc/profile*; then echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> /etc/profile fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: Setting umask via /etc/profile and /etc/profile.d is a standard hardening control with minimal risk of lockout; numeric values can be safely updated.

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi
umask 077
umask 022

review_feedback: The proposed remediation is only a high-level plan and has not been executed; consequently the vulnerability remains unresolved. Suggestions: Explicitly define and validate UMASK_VAL (e.g., UMASK_VAL="022") before use.; Iterate over /etc/profile.d/*.sh with proper quoting using find -print0 and xargs -0.; Whitelist allowed umask values to prevent permissive defaults.; Replace umask lines only after confirming they match the pattern and preserve indentation.; Run shell syntax checks (bash -n or shellcheck) on each edited file before proceeding.; Add an idempotent check to modify a file only if the current umask differs from the target.; Perform verification with a temporary test user to ensure the umask propagates correctly.; Record changes and retain backups for rollback.

concerns: The remediation script contains syntax errors and undefined variables (e.g., var_accounts_user_umask may be empty).
It uses an rpm kernel check that may not be relevant to the umask hardening requirement, adding unnecessary complexity.
The backup step may fail on permission‑restricted files and does not handle all edge cases (e.g., already present umask lines, commented lines).
The script does not validate that the inserted umask value conforms to policy before applying it.
There is no verification step to ensure the modified files are syntactically correct or that the umask is correctly propagated to new shells.
suggested_improvements: Explicitly set the desired umask value (e.g., UMASK_VAL="022") and validate it before use.
Iterate over all files in /etc/profile.d with proper quoting and handle spaces in filenames.
Use a whitelist or policy‑driven approach to avoid overwriting intentionally different umask settings.
Insert or replace the umask line only after confirming it does not break shell syntax (e.g., using shellcheck or bash -n).
Add a final verification step that reads the umask from a test user session.
Integrate the changes into a configuration management tool for idempotent enforcement.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The proposed remediation is only a high-level plan and has not been executed; consequently the vulnerability remains unresolved.
concerns: No actual changes have been applied; the described steps have not been run.
The script uses undefined variables (e.g., var_accounts_user_umask) and lacks a concrete umask value setting.
Backup step may fail on permission‑restricted files and does not robustly handle existing umask lines.
No validation that the umask value conforms to policy before insertion.
No syntax validation of edited files (bash -n / shellcheck).
No verification step to confirm the umask is correctly set for new sessions.
Risk of overwriting legitimate, intentionally different umask settings without policy checks.
suggested_improvements: Explicitly define and validate UMASK_VAL (e.g., UMASK_VAL="022") before use.
Iterate over /etc/profile.d/*.sh with proper quoting using find -print0 and xargs -0.
Whitelist allowed umask values to prevent permissive defaults.
Replace umask lines only after confirming they match the pattern and preserve indentation.
Run shell syntax checks (bash -n or shellcheck) on each edited file before proceeding.
Add an idempotent check to modify a file only if the current umask differs from the target.
Perform verification with a temporary test user to ensure the umask propagates correctly.
Record changes and retain backups for rollback.
